label: Title
verbatim: Does a Global Perspective Help Prune Sparse MoEs Elegantly?
paraphrase: Paper title. The acronym GRAPE expands to 'Global Redundancy-Aware Pruning of Experts'.
consequences: Defines the GRAPE method that the spec's Stage 1 budget allocation cites.

label: Abstract
verbatim: We propose GRAPE (Global Redundancy-Aware Pruning of Experts), a global pruning strategy that dynamically allocates pruning budgets based on cross-layer redundancy.
paraphrase: GRAPE allocates pruning budget per layer based on cross-layer redundancy rather than uniformly.
preconditions: Sparse MoE model with multiple MoE layers.
consequences: Different layers receive different pruning budgets.

label: Abstract
verbatim: Experiments on Mixtral-8x7B, Mixtral-8x22B, DeepSeek-MoE, Qwen-MoE, and GPT-OSS show that, under the same pruning budget, GRAPE consistently achieves the best average performance.
paraphrase: GRAPE evaluated on Mixtral-8x7B/22B, DeepSeek-MoE, Qwen-MoE, and GPT-OSS.
preconditions: Same total pruning budget across methods.
consequences: GRAPE outperforms uniform layer-wise baselines on these models.

label: Abstract
verbatim: On the three main models reported in the paper, it improves average accuracy over the strongest local baseline by 1.40% on average across pruning settings, with gains of up to 2.45%.
paraphrase: Average gain over strongest local baseline is 1.40%, max gain 2.45%.
preconditions: Three main models, multiple pruning settings.
consequences: Quantifies expected GRAPE improvement.

label: Eq. 1
verbatim: y^l = sum_{s_i in S} alpha^l_{s_i} * phi^l_{s_i}(x)
paraphrase: Output of the l-th MoE layer is a routing-coefficient-weighted sum over activated experts S = {s_1,...,s_N}.
preconditions: S is the set of activated experts; phi^l_{s_i} is the s_i-th expert in layer l; alpha^l_{s_i} is its routing coefficient.
consequences: Defines MoE layer output formula for the rest of the paper.

label: Expert architecture
verbatim: Each expert phi^l(.) consists of two linear layers with a GeLU activation in between.
paraphrase: Each expert is a two-linear-layer MLP with GeLU non-linearity.
consequences: Defines the unit being pruned/merged.

label: Similarity matrix D^l
verbatim: Let D^l in R^{N x N} denote the pairwise similarity matrix of experts in the l-th MoE layer, where D^l_{ij} measures the similarity between expert i and expert j. In practice, D^l can be instantiated using CKA (Davari et al.), mean squared error, or other similarity measures.
paraphrase: Per-layer pairwise similarity matrix D^l. Implementations may use CKA, MSE, or other similarity metrics.
consequences: Spec's Stage 1 must choose a similarity metric (CKA / MSE / etc.) consistent with the paper.

label: Eq. 2
paraphrase: Average intra-layer redundancy R^l is the mean off-diagonal pairwise similarity, normalized by N(N-1).
preconditions: D^l is the N x N similarity matrix for layer l.
consequences: Defines AVERAGE intra-layer redundancy used for visualization (Fig. 1).

label: Eq. 3
verbatim: tilde{R}^l = (R^l - min_{l'} R^{l'}) / (max_{l'} R^{l'} - min_{l'} R^{l'})
paraphrase: Normalized cross-layer redundancy tilde{R}^l in [0,1] is min-max normalization of R^l across layers.
consequences: Comparable cross-layer redundancy values; larger tilde{R}^l means more redundant layer.

label: Cross-layer variation
verbatim: expert redundancy varies substantially across layers within the same model. In general, earlier MoE layers tend to exhibit lower redundancy than later ones. However, this trend is not strictly monotonic, as some intermediate layers also display relatively low redundancy.
paraphrase: Earlier layers tend to be less redundant than later ones; trend is non-monotonic.
preconditions: Visualization in Fig. 1 for Mixtral-8x22B and Deepseek-MoE.
consequences: Motivates non-uniform pruning budget.

label: Eq. 4
verbatim: A = blockdiag(D^1, D^2, ..., D^L) in R^{LN x LN}
paraphrase: Block-diagonal global similarity matrix A formed from per-layer D^l blocks.
preconditions: L MoE layers, N experts each.
consequences: Permits a global formulation of the pruning objective.

label: Pruning target
verbatim: Our objective is to reduce the total number of experts to a target value K < LN by pruning structurally redundant experts. This corresponds to globally pruning exactly LN - K experts.
paraphrase: Total expert budget is K (out of LN); LN - K experts must be pruned/merged globally.
preconditions: Layers L, experts N per layer.
consequences: Defines the global budget constraint enforced by Algorithm 1.

label: Eq. 5
verbatim: argmin_M sum_{i != j} A_{ij} * M_{ij}, where M = blockdiag(M^1, M^2, ..., M^L) in {0,1}^{LN x LN}
paraphrase: Naive objective: minimize total residual similarity over a block-diagonal binary mask.
preconditions: M block-diagonal binary mask of remaining experts.
consequences: Direct optimization may collapse all pruning into a few highly redundant layers (motivates entropy regularization).

label: Degenerate solution
verbatim: directly optimizing eq. (5) may lead to a degenerate solution... certain layers, such as the final layer of DeepseekMoE, exhibit disproportionately high redundancy. In such cases, the pruning budget may be allocated excessively to only a few highly redundant layers, causing severe layer imbalance and even model collapse.
paraphrase: Pure greedy minimization of Eq. 5 can over-prune one layer (e.g. final DeepSeek-MoE layer), causing collapse.
consequences: Motivates entropy-based regularization / safeguard.

label: Eq. 6
verbatim: p_l = I(D^l (.) M^l) / I(A (.) M)
paraphrase: Layer fraction p_l = (count of remaining experts in layer l) / (count of remaining experts in whole model), where I(.) counts remaining experts; sum_l p_l = 1.
consequences: Provides per-layer retention distribution for entropy.

label: Eq. 7
verbatim: E = - sum_l p_l log p_l
paraphrase: Global entropy E of the layer-wise retention distribution {p_l}.
consequences: Higher E = more even allocation; lower E = pruning concentrated in few layers.

label: Eq. 8
paraphrase: In the clustering view, p_l is the fraction of clusters in layer l versus total clusters; each cluster equals one retained expert.
preconditions: Initially each expert is a singleton cluster.
consequences: Operational definition of p_l used during greedy pruning.

label: Eq. 9
verbatim: E = - sum_l p_l log p_l
paraphrase: Same Shannon entropy as Eq. 7 but applied to cluster-fraction view (Eq. 8).
consequences: Used as the live entropy measurement updated each iteration.

label: Eq. 10
verbatim: E_b = E (1 - gamma)
paraphrase: Entropy threshold E_b = (1 - gamma) * initial unpruned entropy E, where gamma in [0,1] is entropy tolerance.
preconditions: E computed at initialization (each expert singleton); gamma in [0,1].
consequences: Layer is frozen when post-merge entropy falls below E_b.

label: gamma (entropy tolerance)
verbatim: where gamma in [0,1] is an entropy tolerance parameter. A larger gamma allows more imbalance across layers during pruning, while a smaller gamma enforces a more even allocation of retained experts.
paraphrase: Hyperparameter gamma in [0,1] controls layer-imbalance tolerance: larger = more imbalance allowed.
consequences: Spec's Stage 1 must specify a value for gamma; paper does not pin a specific value in main text.

label: Eq. 11
verbatim: R^l = sum_{i != j} D^l_{ij}
paraphrase: Layer-redundancy score R^l in Eq. 11 is the SUM of off-diagonal pairwise similarities for layer l (NOT the mean as in Eq. 2). 'quantifies the total residual similarity mass in layer l.'
preconditions: D^l is the layer-l similarity matrix; entries D^l_{ij} for the *current* clustering (zeros after a merge per Algorithm 1 line 9).
consequences: CRITICAL: Eq. 11 is a SUM, not a mean. The spec at §4 Stage 1 cites 'Eq. 11 — sum, not mean'. Algorithm 1 selects layer with largest residual R^l at each iteration; R^l is updated by R^l <- R^l - 2 D^l_{i*,j*} after each merge.

label: Eq. 11 vs Eq. 2 distinction
paraphrase: Paper uses two different formulas both denoted R^l: Eq. 2 (average redundancy, used for visualization in Fig. 1 and normalization in Eq. 3) is divided by N(N-1); Eq. 11 (residual redundancy, used in Algorithm 1) is a raw sum without normalization.
consequences: Implementation must use SUM (Eq. 11) for the algorithm's layer selection. Using the mean would change layer-selection behavior.

label: Algorithm 1 (Require/Ensure)
verbatim: Require: Similarity blocks {D^l}_{l=1}^L, target experts K, entropy tolerance gamma. Ensure: Clusters {C^l}_{l=1}^L s.t. sum_l |C^l| = K
paraphrase: Algorithm 1 inputs: similarity blocks, target K, gamma. Output: clusters whose sizes sum to K.
consequences: Defines exact API for the GRAPE allocation routine.

label: Algorithm 1 line 1
verbatim: C^l <- {{0}, ..., {N-1}}, R^l <- sum_{i != j} D^l_{ij} for all l
paraphrase: Initialization: each expert is its own singleton cluster; R^l initialized to sum (Eq. 11) of off-diagonal D^l entries per layer.
consequences: Initial state for the greedy loop.

label: Algorithm 1 line 2
verbatim: E <- Entropy({C^l}), E_b <- E(1 - gamma), F <- empty
paraphrase: Compute initial entropy E (all singletons), set threshold E_b per Eq. 10, and initialize empty frozen-set F.
consequences: Threshold E_b is fixed for the entire run.

label: Algorithm 1 line 3
verbatim: while sum_l |C^l| > K do
paraphrase: Loop until total cluster count equals target K.
consequences: Termination condition for greedy merging.

label: Algorithm 1 lines 4-5 (restart)
verbatim: if F = {1,...,L} then F <- empty // restart
paraphrase: If all layers are frozen but target K not yet reached, reset frozen-set F to empty and continue.
preconditions: All L layers were previously frozen by entropy threshold.
consequences: Ensures the algorithm always reaches target budget K even when the entropy floor would otherwise block all layers.

label: Algorithm 1 line 6
verbatim: l* <- argmax_{l not in F} R^l
paraphrase: Among unfrozen layers, pick the layer with maximum residual redundancy R^l (per Eq. 11 sum).
consequences: Greedy layer selection step.

label: Algorithm 1 line 7
verbatim: (i*, j*) <- argmax_{i != j} D^{l*}_{ij}
paraphrase: Within the chosen layer, pick the most similar pair of experts (i*, j*).
consequences: Greedy intra-layer pair selection.

label: Algorithm 1 line 8
verbatim: C^{l*} <- Union(C^{l*}, i*, j*)
paraphrase: Merge clusters containing i* and j* in layer l*.
consequences: Reduces total cluster count by 1.

label: Algorithm 1 line 9
paraphrase: After merging, zero the (i*,j*) and (j*,i*) entries of the similarity matrix to prevent re-selection.
consequences: Update keeps off-diagonal mass consistent with merged state.

label: Algorithm 1 line 10
paraphrase: Decrement layer redundancy R^{l*} by twice the merged-pair similarity (subtracting both off-diagonal entries).
consequences: Maintains R^l consistent with the SUM definition of Eq. 11. Confirms Eq. 11 is a sum, not mean — a mean update would have a 1/(N(N-1)) factor.

label: Algorithm 1 line 11
verbatim: E <- Entropy({C^l})
paraphrase: Recompute global entropy using current cluster fractions (Eq. 8/9).
consequences: Used for freeze decision.

label: Algorithm 1 lines 12-13
verbatim: if E < E_b then F <- F union {l*} ▷ freeze
paraphrase: If post-merge entropy falls below threshold E_b, freeze layer l* (no further pruning until restart).
consequences: Prevents over-concentration of pruning into few layers.

label: Algorithm 1 line 14
verbatim: return {C^l}_{l=1}^L
paraphrase: Return final clustering with sum_l |C^l| = K.
consequences: Output: per-layer expert clusters defining the merge plan.

label: One-shot greedy
verbatim: GRAPE performs one-shot entropy-aware greedy pruning with restart, as summarized in algorithm 1.
paraphrase: GRAPE is one-shot (not iterative training) and uses greedy pruning with entropy-aware restart.
consequences: Implementation should run a single pass to produce K clusters; no fine-tuning loop.

label: Mixtral-8x22B architecture
verbatim: Each MoE layer in the Mixtral model contains 8 experts, with 2 experts activated per token. Mixtral-8x22B consist of 56 layers.
paraphrase: Mixtral-8x22B: 56 MoE layers, 8 experts each, top-2 routing.
consequences: Determines LN = 56 * 8 = 448 total experts.

label: DeepSeek-MoE architecture
verbatim: Deepseek-MoE-16B contains 27 MoE layers, each comprising 64 private experts and 2 shared experts. For each token, 6 of the 64 private experts and the 2 shared experts are activated.
paraphrase: DeepSeek-MoE-16B: 27 MoE layers; 64 private + 2 shared experts per layer; 6 private + 2 shared activated per token.
consequences: GRAPE prunes among the 64 private experts per layer (shared experts always active).

label: GPT-oss-20b architecture
verbatim: GPT-oss (gpt-oss-20b) has 24 MoE layers, where 4 of 32 experts in each layer are activated for each token.
paraphrase: GPT-oss-20b: 24 MoE layers, 32 experts each, top-4 routing.
consequences: GPT-oss tested under 2e/4e pruning settings (i.e., remove 2 or 4 experts per layer baseline).

label: Baselines
verbatim: We compare GRAPE with four recent MoE pruning approaches (He et al., 2024; Li et al., 2024a; Lu et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2025).
paraphrase: Baselines: router-guided (Li 2024a), count-guided (He 2024 / frequency), Enumerate (Lu 2024 loss-based), DEK (Zhang 2025).
consequences: Defines comparison set.

label: Uniform-vs-global
verbatim: All baseline methods perform uniform pruning by removing the same number of experts from each MoE layer. In contrast, our method maintains the same overall pruning budget but adaptively determines the number of experts to prune in each layer according to layer-wise redundancy.
paraphrase: Baselines: uniform per-layer pruning. GRAPE: same total budget, layer-adaptive allocation.
preconditions: Same total expert budget across methods.
consequences: Like-for-like comparison ensures gains attributable to allocation strategy.

label: Task-agnostic / no fine-tune
verbatim: To ensure a fair comparison in the task-agnostic setting, we disable all fine-tuning stages for all methods.
paraphrase: All methods evaluated without post-pruning fine-tuning, in task-agnostic mode.
consequences: Reported numbers are zero-shot post-pruning.

label: GPT-OSS evaluation
verbatim: GPT-OSS follows its default reasoning style, where the model first produces intermediate reasoning steps before generating the final answer. We set the reasoning effort of GPT-OSS to medium and randomly sample 1000 examples from MMLU for evaluation due to computational cost.
paraphrase: GPT-OSS uses default chain-of-thought reasoning (medium effort); MMLU evaluated on 1000-sample subset due to cost; full eval sets used for other datasets.
consequences: Reproduction must match reasoning effort and MMLU subsetting for GPT-OSS.

label: Mixtral/DeepSeek eval
verbatim: For Mixtral and DeepSeek-MoE, the models are prompted to directly generate the final answer... For Mixtral and DeepSeek-MoE, we evaluate on the full MMLU dataset.
paraphrase: Mixtral/DeepSeek: direct-answer prompting; MMLU evaluated on full dataset.
consequences: Reproduction protocol differs from GPT-OSS.

label: Table 1 Mixtral-8x22B original
paraphrase: Mixtral-8x22B baseline accuracies: MMLU Humanities 68.6, Social 84.1, STEM 67.1, Other 78.7, BoolQ 87.9, OpenBookQA 35.8, RTE 71.2, Avg 70.4.
consequences: Reference for retained-performance metric.

label: Table 1 Mixtral-8x22B GRAPE
paraphrase: Mixtral-8x22B GRAPE 2e/4e: avg 68.2 / 62.6.
preconditions: 2 or 4 experts pruned per layer (uniform-equivalent total budget).
consequences: Demonstrates gain over local DEK baseline (67.0 / 61.1).

label: Table 1 DeepSeek-MoE GRAPE
paraphrase: DeepSeek-MoE GRAPE 2e/4e: avg 49.8 / 49.5.
preconditions: 2 or 4 private experts pruned per layer (uniform-equivalent total).
consequences: DeepSeek gains smaller (1.84% / 1.43%) due to severe redundancy imbalance.

label: Table 2 GPT-OSS GRAPE
paraphrase: GPT-OSS GRAPE 2e/4e: avg 90.3 / 89.5.
preconditions: 2 or 4 experts pruned per layer (uniform-equivalent total).
consequences: Gains over local baseline 0.44% (2e) / 0.45% (4e).

label: Mixtral-8x22B gains
verbatim: On Mixtral-8x22B, GRAPE reaches average accuracies of 68.2 (2e) and 62.6 (4e), outperforming the strongest local baseline by 1.79% and 2.45%, respectively.
paraphrase: Gains over strongest local baseline: +1.79 (2e), +2.45 (4e) on Mixtral-8x22B.
consequences: Largest reported gains.

label: DeepSeek-MoE gains
verbatim: On DeepSeek-MoE, the gains are smaller but consistent, with GRAPE achieving 49.8 (2e) and 49.5 (4e), corresponding to relative improvements of 1.84% and 1.43% over the strongest local baseline.
paraphrase: Gains: +1.84% / +1.43% relative on DeepSeek-MoE.
consequences: Smaller because DeepSeek has high last-layer redundancy.

label: GPT-OSS gains
verbatim: On GPT-OSS, GRAPE obtains 90.3 (2e) and 89.5 (4e), improving over the strongest local baseline by 0.44% and 0.45%, respectively.
paraphrase: Gains: +0.44 / +0.45 absolute over strongest local baseline on GPT-OSS.
consequences: Smaller absolute gain in low-redundancy regime.

label: Collapse caveat
verbatim: experiments on Deepseek-MoE reveal that severe imbalance in layerwise redundancy can cause global pruning to collapse the model.
paraphrase: Severe layer-redundancy imbalance can collapse the model under global pruning; entropy safeguard mitigates but does not eliminate this risk.
consequences: Practitioners should monitor per-layer allocation; gamma tuning may be required.

label: Appendix A models
verbatim: This section provides additional experimental results on Mixtral-8x7B and Qwen-MoE, which are omitted from the main text. We follow the same experimental protocol and evaluation metric as in the main experiments and report results using retained performance, defined as the ratio between the accuracy of the pruned model and that of the original model.
paraphrase: Appendix A reports Mixtral-8x7B and Qwen-MoE under retained-performance metric (pruned/original accuracy ratio).
consequences: Defines retained-performance ratio metric.

label: Global Greedy alias
verbatim: Global Greedy achieves the highest or near-highest retained performance across tasks for both models.
paraphrase: Appendix uses 'Global Greedy' as alias for GRAPE in plots.
consequences: Naming consistency: 'Global Greedy' = GRAPE in figures.

label: Mixtral-8x7B 4e observation
verbatim: On Mixtral-8x7B, the advantage of Global Greedy becomes particularly clear under four-expert pruning, where uniform layer-wise pruning methods suffer substantial retention drops, especially on MMLU.
paraphrase: GRAPE's advantage is largest on Mixtral-8x7B under 4e pruning, especially MMLU.
preconditions: Mixtral-8x7B with 4 experts pruned per layer (uniform-equivalent total budget).
consequences: GRAPE most useful at higher pruning rates.

label: Qwen-MoE retention
verbatim: On QwenMoE, where pruning is generally less destructive, Global Greedy still provides consistently strong retention across all tasks and maintains the best overall average performance.
paraphrase: On Qwen-MoE GRAPE remains best on overall average even though pruning is less destructive in this model family.
consequences: GRAPE robust across model families.

label: Method taxonomy
verbatim: Many studies have explored effective strategies for pruning MoEs, which can be broadly categorized into four types: visiting frequency-guided (Chen et al., 2022; He et al., 2024), router-guided (Li et al., 2024a), search-based (Lu et al., 2024), and feature-based (Zhang et al., 2025) methods.
paraphrase: Existing MoE pruning methods fall into 4 categories: frequency-guided, router-guided, search-based, feature-based.
consequences: GRAPE differs by adding global cross-layer allocation orthogonal to these intra-layer schemes.

label: Prior uniformity gap
verbatim: these methods typically allocate the pruning budget uniformly across all layers, ignoring inter-layer variation in sparsity.
paraphrase: Prior methods uniformly allocate budget across layers, ignoring inter-layer redundancy variation.
consequences: Defines GRAPE's contribution: layer-adaptive budget.

label: Figure 1
verbatim: Figure 1: Cross-layer redundancy of different MoE models, including Mixtral-8x22B and Deepseek-MoE.
paraphrase: Fig. 1 visualizes per-layer redundancy R^l (Eq. 2 averaged form) across layers for Mixtral-8x22B and DeepSeek-MoE.
consequences: Visual evidence of cross-layer heterogeneity.

label: DeepSeek last-layer redundancy
verbatim: certain layers, such as the final layer of DeepseekMoE, exhibit disproportionately high redundancy.
paraphrase: DeepSeek-MoE's final layer is disproportionately redundant; greedy un-regularized pruning concentrates there.
consequences: Justifies entropy regularization.

label: Entropy interpretation
verbatim: A larger entropy indicates that the retained experts are distributed more evenly across layers, whereas a smaller entropy indicates that pruning is overly concentrated in only a few layers.
paraphrase: High entropy = even cross-layer retention; low entropy = pruning concentrated in few layers.
consequences: Interpretation guide for entropy threshold.

label: Cluster -> retained expert
verbatim: we maintain the current clustering structure {C^l}_{l=1}^L, where C^l denotes the current set of expert clusters in layer l, and each cluster corresponds to one retained expert after merging.
paraphrase: Each cluster after merging is one retained expert; |C^l| = number of remaining experts in layer l.
consequences: Operational meaning: GRAPE produces merge groups, not deletions per se.

label: Entropy as safeguard, not regularizer
verbatim: rather than directly optimizing an entropy-regularized objective, GRAPE uses global entropy as a safeguard in a greedy pruning procedure.
paraphrase: Entropy is a hard safeguard (freeze trigger), not a soft regularization term added to the loss.
consequences: Implementation must use the freeze/restart pattern, not gradient-based regularized optimization.

label: GRAPE objective summary
verbatim: Unlike layer-wise pruning methods that remove a fixed number of experts from each layer independently, our approach jointly determines expert merging across all layers under a unified pruning budget.
paraphrase: GRAPE makes a joint cross-layer decision under a single global budget K.
consequences: Unified-budget formulation contrasted with per-layer fixed quotas.

label: Strongest local baseline = DEK
verbatim: DEK (Zhang et al., 2025) 62.3/57.8 ... 67.0/61.1 (Mixtral-8x22B avg)
paraphrase: DEK is the strongest local baseline on Mixtral-8x22B with avg 67.0 (2e) and 61.1 (4e); GRAPE beats it by ~1.2 / ~1.5 points.
consequences: Identifies DEK as the reference baseline.

label: Mixtral-8x22B original avg
verbatim: Original (Jiang et al., 2024) ... 70.4 (Mixtral-8x22B)
paraphrase: Unpruned Mixtral-8x22B average accuracy: 70.4%.
consequences: Reference for retained performance.

label: DeepSeek-MoE original avg
verbatim: Original (Dai et al., 2024) ... 50.0 (Deepseek-MoE)
paraphrase: Unpruned DeepSeek-MoE average accuracy: 50.0%.
consequences: Reference for retained performance.

label: GPT-OSS original avg
verbatim: Original (Dai et al., 2024) 85.6 88.7 93.0 92.8 90.0 (GPT-oss)
paraphrase: Unpruned GPT-OSS average accuracy: 90.0%.
consequences: Reference for retained performance.

label: 2e/4e notation
verbatim: Each cell reports accuracy in the format 2e/4e, where we denote e as the number of experts to prune in each layer.
paraphrase: Notation '2e/4e' = 2 experts pruned per layer / 4 experts pruned per layer (defining the total global budget for GRAPE comparison).
consequences: GRAPE's global budget K is set so that LN - K equals 2L or 4L (uniform-equivalent total).

label: Shared experts not pruned
verbatim: each comprising 64 private experts and 2 shared experts. For each token, 6 of the 64 private experts and the 2 shared experts are activated.
paraphrase: Implicit: DeepSeek's 2 shared experts per layer are always active; GRAPE prunes among the 64 private experts only.
consequences: Per-layer N for DeepSeek = 64 (private experts), not 66.
